name: Tethered Cord Syndrome
description: A neurological disorder caused by tissue attachments that limit the movement of the spinal cord within the spinal column. Often presents in childhood with progressive neurological and urological symptoms.
keyInfo: The condition that inspired this tool — a mother used AI to identify it after 17 doctors missed it.

name: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (Hypermobile type)
description: A connective tissue disorder characterized by joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility. The most common type of EDS.
keyInfo: Often missed for years. Average time to diagnosis is 10-12 years. Commonly misdiagnosed as 'growing pains' or 'just flexible'.

name: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
description: A form of dysautonomia characterized by excessive heart rate increase upon standing, often with lightheadedness, fainting, and fatigue.
keyInfo: Frequently dismissed as anxiety. Often co-occurs with EDS and MCAS (the 'trifecta').

name: Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS)
description: A condition in which mast cells release excessive chemical mediators, causing symptoms across multiple organ systems including skin, GI, cardiovascular, and neurological.
keyInfo: Part of the EDS-POTS-MCAS 'trifecta'. Symptoms can look like allergies but standard allergy tests may be negative.

name: Wilson Disease
description: A genetic disorder of copper metabolism leading to copper accumulation in the liver, brain, and other organs. Treatable if caught early.
keyInfo: Highly treatable if diagnosed early. Kayser-Fleischer rings in the eyes are characteristic but not always present.

name: Marfan Syndrome
description: A connective tissue disorder affecting the heart, blood vessels, bones, joints, and eyes. Caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene.
keyInfo: Aortic root dilation is the main life-threatening complication. Regular cardiac monitoring is essential.

name: Neurofibromatosis Type 1
description: A genetic condition causing tumors to grow along nerves, with café-au-lait spots, freckling, and potential learning difficulties.
keyInfo: Six or more café-au-lait spots larger than 5mm in children is a diagnostic criterion.

name: Turner Syndrome
description: A chromosomal condition affecting females, caused by complete or partial absence of one X chromosome.
keyInfo: Short stature and delayed puberty are the most common presenting features.

name: Williams Syndrome
description: A developmental disorder affecting the heart, blood vessels, and many other organ systems, often with a characteristic facial appearance and outgoing personality.
keyInfo: Characteristic 'elfin' facial features and unusually friendly personality. Often have musical talent despite intellectual disability.

name: Angelman Syndrome
description: A neurogenetic disorder characterized by severe intellectual disability, speech impairment, seizures, and a characteristically happy demeanor with frequent laughing.
keyInfo: Frequent laughter and happy demeanor are characteristic. Seizures typically begin between ages 1-3.

name: Osteogenesis Imperfecta (Type I)
description: A genetic condition causing extremely fragile bones that break easily, often with minimal or no trauma. Type I is the mildest form.
keyInfo: Blue sclerae (blue-tinted whites of eyes) is a classic sign. Fractures may be mistaken for child abuse.

name: Prader-Willi Syndrome
description: A complex genetic condition causing insatiable hunger, intellectual disability, short stature, and behavioral problems.
keyInfo: Switches from poor feeding in infancy to insatiable hunger in childhood. Early intervention significantly improves outcomes.

name: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex
description: A genetic condition causing non-cancerous tumors in the brain, kidneys, heart, lungs, and skin.
keyInfo: White patches on skin (ash-leaf spots) visible under UV light are often the earliest sign.

name: Noonan Syndrome
description: A genetic disorder characterized by unusual facial features, short stature, heart defects, and developmental delays.
keyInfo: Sometimes called 'male Turner syndrome' though it affects all genders. Heart defects (especially pulmonic stenosis) are common.

name: 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome (DiGeorge)
description: A condition caused by deletion of a small piece of chromosome 22, causing heart defects, immune deficiency, cleft palate, and developmental delays.
keyInfo: Most common microdeletion syndrome. Psychiatric issues (anxiety, ADHD) are common in adolescence. Previously known as DiGeorge syndrome.

name: Cystic Fibrosis
description: A genetic disease causing thick, sticky mucus that clogs the lungs and obstructs the pancreas.
keyInfo: Now screened for at birth in most countries. New CFTR modulators (like Trikafta) have dramatically improved outcomes.

name: Phenylketonuria (PKU)
description: A metabolic disorder where the body cannot break down phenylalanine, an amino acid. Untreated, it causes intellectual disability.
keyInfo: Screened at birth via heel prick test. Completely manageable with diet. One of the success stories of newborn screening.

name: Rett Syndrome
description: A rare genetic neurological disorder affecting mainly girls, causing progressive loss of motor skills and speech after initial normal development.
keyInfo: Characteristic hand-wringing movements. Period of regression after initially normal development is a key feature.

name: Fragile X Syndrome
description: The most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and autism, caused by a mutation in the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome.
keyInfo: Prominent ears and long face are characteristic in males. Many carriers have milder symptoms.

name: Gaucher Disease (Type 1)
description: A lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficiency of glucocerebrosidase, leading to accumulation of lipids in various organs.
keyInfo: More common in Ashkenazi Jewish population. Enzyme replacement therapy is available and effective.

name: Fabry Disease
description: A lysosomal storage disorder causing progressive damage to the kidneys, heart, and nervous system due to alpha-galactosidase A deficiency.
keyInfo: Burning pain in hands and feet (acroparesthesias) in childhood is often the first symptom. Enzyme replacement available.

name: Pompe Disease
description: A glycogen storage disorder caused by acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency, leading to progressive muscle weakness.
keyInfo: Infantile form is life-threatening without treatment. Late-onset form causes progressive limb-girdle weakness.

name: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
description: A genetic neuromuscular disease causing motor neurons to degenerate, leading to progressive muscle weakness and atrophy.
keyInfo: New gene therapies (Zolgensma, Spinraza) have transformed outcomes. Early treatment before symptom onset is critical.

name: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy
description: A severe form of muscular dystrophy causing progressive muscle degeneration and weakness, primarily affecting boys.
keyInfo: Gowers' sign (using hands to 'walk up' the legs when standing) is characteristic. Early steroid treatment extends ambulation.

name: Huntington Disease
description: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing motor, cognitive, and psychiatric symptoms.
keyInfo: Juvenile form (under 20) accounts for ~5% of cases and may present with rigidity rather than chorea.

name: Chiari Malformation Type I
description: A structural defect where the cerebellar tonsils extend into the spinal canal, often causing headaches and neurological symptoms.
keyInfo: Often found incidentally on MRI. Frequently co-occurs with EDS and tethered cord. Headaches worse with coughing/straining.

name: Fanconi Anemia
description: A rare genetic condition affecting the bone marrow, causing decreased production of all blood cells and increased cancer risk.
keyInfo: Short stature and thumb/radial anomalies are common early signs. Bone marrow failure develops progressively.

name: Stickler Syndrome
description: A connective tissue disorder affecting collagen, causing eye problems, hearing loss, joint hypermobility, and characteristic facial features.
keyInfo: Retinal detachment risk is significant. Pierre Robin sequence (small jaw, cleft palate) is common at birth.

name: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1
description: A hereditary condition predisposing to tumors of the parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic islet cells.
keyInfo: Hypercalcemia from hyperparathyroidism is often the first sign. Genetic testing allows presymptomatic diagnosis.

name: Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1
description: The most common adult muscular dystrophy, causing progressive muscle weakness, myotonia (difficulty relaxing muscles), and multi-system involvement.
keyInfo: Genetic anticipation — disease severity increases in successive generations. Congenital form can be severe.

name: Raine Syndrome
description: An extremely rare bone disorder characterized by generalized osteosclerosis with periosteal bone formation.
keyInfo: Originally described as lethal, but milder surviving cases are now recognized.

name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis
description: A genetic condition causing excessive iron absorption, leading to iron overload in organs. Very treatable if caught early.
keyInfo: One of the most common genetic diseases in people of European descent. Simple blood test (ferritin, transferrin saturation) can screen.

name: Friedreich Ataxia
description: A progressive neurological condition causing difficulty walking, loss of coordination, and heart disease.
keyInfo: Most common hereditary ataxia. Cardiomyopathy is the leading cause of death. New treatments emerging.

name: Hereditary Angioedema
description: A rare condition causing recurrent episodes of severe swelling in various body parts including the airway.
keyInfo: Swelling episodes can be life-threatening if airway is involved. Does NOT respond to typical allergy medications.

name: Mitochondrial Complex I Deficiency
description: A mitochondrial disease affecting energy production in cells, particularly impacting high-energy organs like the brain, heart, and muscles.
keyInfo: Highly variable presentation. Elevated lactate is a key lab finding. Affects high-energy demand tissues.

name: Achondroplasia
description: The most common form of short-limbed dwarfism, caused by a mutation in the FGFR3 gene.
keyInfo: 80% of cases are new mutations. Vosoritide (Voxzogo) is now approved for growth in children.

name: Loeys-Dietz Syndrome
description: A connective tissue disorder similar to Marfan syndrome but with distinct features including arterial tortuosity and aneurysms.
keyInfo: More aggressive vascular disease than Marfan. Bifid uvula is a distinctive clinical clue.

name: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT)
description: A vascular disorder causing abnormal blood vessel formation, leading to nosebleeds and potentially dangerous arteriovenous malformations.
keyInfo: Recurrent nosebleeds are often the earliest symptom. Lung and brain AVMs can cause strokes if untreated.

name: Maple Syrup Urine Disease
description: A metabolic disorder where the body cannot process certain amino acids, leading to sweet-smelling urine and potentially severe neurological damage.
keyInfo: Named for characteristic maple syrup odor of urine. Now part of newborn screening in many countries.

name: Glycogen Storage Disease Type I (Von Gierke)
description: A metabolic disorder caused by glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency, leading to excess glycogen storage in liver and kidneys.
keyInfo: Hepatomegaly with a protuberant abdomen is characteristic. Frequent feeding is essential to prevent hypoglycemia.

name: CHARGE Syndrome
description: A complex genetic condition affecting multiple organ systems: Coloboma, Heart defects, Atresia choanae, Retardation of growth, Genital abnormalities, Ear abnormalities.
keyInfo: Combination of coloboma and choanal atresia is highly suggestive. Most cases are caused by CHD7 mutations.

name: Alagille Syndrome
description: A genetic disorder affecting the liver, heart, and other organs, characterized by bile duct paucity.
keyInfo: Characteristic facial features (prominent forehead, deep-set eyes) become more apparent with age. Butterfly vertebrae on X-ray.

name: Galactosemia
description: A metabolic disorder where the body cannot properly process galactose, a sugar found in milk.
keyInfo: Symptoms begin within days of milk feeding. Screened at birth in many countries. Strict dietary galactose restriction required.

name: Hereditary Fructose Intolerance
description: A metabolic disorder caused by aldolase B deficiency, leading to severe reactions when fructose is ingested.
keyInfo: Symptoms appear when fruits, juices, or sucrose are introduced. Children may develop natural aversion to sweet foods.

name: Bardet-Biedl Syndrome
description: A ciliopathy characterized by obesity, retinal degeneration, kidney anomalies, polydactyly, and learning difficulties.
keyInfo: Night blindness and loss of peripheral vision progress throughout childhood. Polydactyly at birth may be an early clue.

name: Homocystinuria
description: A metabolic disorder of methionine metabolism, causing a range of complications including lens dislocation, skeletal abnormalities, and blood clots.
keyInfo: Can mimic Marfan syndrome. Lens dislocation is downward (vs upward in Marfan). B6 supplementation helps some patients.

name: Kabuki Syndrome
description: A congenital disorder with distinctive facial features resembling Kabuki theater makeup, intellectual disability, and skeletal anomalies.
keyInfo: Long palpebral fissures with eversion of lateral third of lower eyelid are characteristic.

name: Cornelia de Lange Syndrome
description: A developmental disorder affecting multiple body systems, characterized by distinctive facial features, growth retardation, and limb defects.
keyInfo: Synophrys (joined eyebrows) and long eyelashes are characteristic. GERD is very common and often severe.

name: Biotinidase Deficiency
description: A treatable metabolic disorder where the body cannot recycle the vitamin biotin, leading to neurological and skin problems.
keyInfo: Completely treatable with daily biotin supplementation. Part of newborn screening in many countries.

name: Hereditary Sensory and Autonomic Neuropathy
description: A group of genetic disorders affecting sensory and autonomic nerves, causing loss of sensation and autonomic dysfunction.
keyInfo: Painless injuries and fractures may be the presenting sign. Some types include anhidrosis (inability to sweat).

name: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
description: A genetic condition predisposing to lung disease (emphysema) and liver disease due to deficiency of the alpha-1 antitrypsin protein.
keyInfo: Often misdiagnosed as COPD or asthma. Simple blood test can detect it. Augmentation therapy is available.

name: Mastocytosis
description: A group of disorders caused by accumulation of mast cells in skin and/or internal organs.
keyInfo: Darier sign (whealing when skin lesions are rubbed) is characteristic. Tryptase levels help with diagnosis.

name: Crouzon Syndrome
description: A genetic disorder characterized by premature fusion of certain skull bones (craniosynostosis), affecting the shape of the head and face.
keyInfo: Prominent eyes due to shallow orbits. Head shape changes may be progressive. Multiple surgeries often needed.

name: Sotos Syndrome
description: A genetic overgrowth syndrome characterized by excessive growth during childhood, distinctive facial features, and learning disabilities.
keyInfo: Advanced bone age is a key finding. Most children's growth normalizes in adulthood.

name: Beckwith-Wiedemann Syndrome
description: An overgrowth syndrome predisposing to childhood tumors, characterized by macroglossia, omphalocele, and gigantism.
keyInfo: Increased risk for Wilms tumor and hepatoblastoma. Tumor surveillance protocol until age 7-8 is standard.

name: Smith-Lemli-Opitz Syndrome
description: A metabolic disorder of cholesterol synthesis causing multiple congenital anomalies and intellectual disability.
keyInfo: 2-3 toe syndactyly is a hallmark feature. Cholesterol supplementation may help. Low cholesterol on labs is a clue.

name: Treacher Collins Syndrome
description: A condition affecting the development of bones and tissues of the face, causing distinctive facial features and sometimes hearing loss.
keyInfo: Downward-slanting eyes and underdeveloped cheekbones are characteristic. Intelligence is typically normal.

name: Waardenburg Syndrome
description: A group of genetic conditions characterized by varying degrees of hearing loss and changes in hair, skin, and eye pigmentation.
keyInfo: Heterochromia (different colored eyes) or brilliant blue eyes with hearing loss is a classic presentation.

name: Cockayne Syndrome
description: A rare disorder characterized by growth failure, photosensitivity, progressive neurological dysfunction, and premature aging.
keyInfo: Extreme sun sensitivity is often an early sign. 'Cachectic dwarfism' with large ears and sunken eyes develops over time.

name: Mucopolysaccharidosis Type I (Hurler)
description: A lysosomal storage disease caused by alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency, leading to progressive multi-organ dysfunction.
keyInfo: Coarsening of facial features over time is characteristic. Early bone marrow transplant can be curative for severe form.

name: Apert Syndrome
description: A genetic syndrome characterized by craniosynostosis and severe syndactyly of the hands and feet.
keyInfo: Mitten-like fusion of fingers is distinctive. Multiple surgeries needed to separate digits and correct skull shape.

name: Tyrosinemia Type I
description: A severe metabolic disorder of tyrosine metabolism primarily affecting the liver and kidneys.
keyInfo: Nitisinone (NTBC) has transformed this from a lethal disease to a manageable one. Cabbage-like odor may be present.

name: Familial Mediterranean Fever
description: An autoinflammatory disease causing recurrent episodes of fever with inflammation of the abdominal lining, joints, or chest.
keyInfo: More common in Mediterranean populations. Colchicine prevents attacks and amyloidosis in most patients.

name: Niemann-Pick Disease Type C
description: A lysosomal storage disorder causing progressive neurological deterioration due to impaired cholesterol trafficking.
keyInfo: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (difficulty looking up/down) is a key diagnostic clue. Often presents with neonatal jaundice.

name: Hereditary Spherocytosis
description: A hemolytic anemia caused by defects in red blood cell membrane proteins, causing sphere-shaped red cells that are destroyed prematurely.
keyInfo: Neonatal jaundice and later gallstones may be presenting features. Splenectomy often dramatically improves symptoms.
